data.title: test-batched.sh: SIGURG mid-run corrupts state file -> subsequent resume restarts from early checkpoint
data.description:
## Incident Overview
During the 2026-04-18 evening session, two consecutive SIGURG (exit 144) signals fired while test-batched.sh was running the bash test suite at 226/301 tests completed. After the SIGURGs, the state file showed only 19 completed tests — dropping ~200 tests worth of progress. This required ~10 additional re-run iterations to reach 301/301.

## Expected Behavior
SIGURG should pause progress at the current test boundary. On resume, test-batched.sh should continue from the last cleanly completed test (226/301 in this case), not from a much earlier checkpoint.

## Actual Behavior
Two consecutive SIGURG signals caused the state file to reset from 226 completed entries to 19. The mechanism appears to be: SIGURG kills the runner mid-write to the state file, leaving a partially-written or earlier snapshot. The next invocation reads the corrupted/stale state and treats it as authoritative.

## Root Cause (hypothesis)
test-batched.sh writes the state file incrementally as each test completes. If SIGURG fires mid-write (during the JSON serialization or file write of a batch update), the state file may be left in a truncated or earlier state. The resume logic reads the file as-is without detecting truncation or comparing against a checksum/sequence number.

## Fix Direction
- Atomic state file writes: write to a temp file then mv (atomic on POSIX) so the state file is never in a partially-written state
- Alternatively: maintain a monotonically increasing sequence counter in the state file; on resume, reject any state file with a lower count than the prior run's high-water mark
